coredata.dc:title: Single Step Green Synthesis of Carbon Dots from Murraya koenigii leaves; A Unique Turn-off Fluorescent contrivance for Selective Sensing of Cd (II) ion
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Pandey, Subhash Chandra
Kumar, Amit
Sahu, Sumanta Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Cadmium ion is the most toxic metal ion among all the hazardous metals; so, its detection is a crucial area for research and diagnosis. In this article, we have synthesized carbon dots (CDs) from natural source [ Murraya koenigii (Curry leaves) leaves] by the hydrothermal method for selective and sensitive detection of Cd2+ ions. The fluorescence quenching of CDs was due to ligand-metal charge transfer (LMCT). The synthesized CDs can donate electrons pair to the excited state vacant d-orbitals of the Cd2+ ions and form a chelate and diminishes the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. The synthesized CDs were characterized by Photoluminescence, UV-visible spectra, zeta potential, XRD, FTIR, HR-TEM, XPS, DLS, and life-time decay analysis. The CDs was highly selective towards Cd2+ ions sensing with broad linear range (0.01-8 μM) and limit of detection 0.29 nM. More importantly, this synthesized CDs successfully detect the Cd2+ ions in real water samples, and also shows high selectivity to Cd2+ ions even in the occurrence of other interfering metal ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Green synthesis
Curry leaves
Cd2+ions sensing
Real water samples analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and analysis of water sample
Cd2+ ions detection by fluorometric method
The pH and ionic strengths stability of CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research work was supported by Indian Institute of Technology (Indian School of Mines), Dhanbad (Jharkhand), India.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The UV-visible spectrum of CDs and after addition of Cd2+ ions, (b) Photoluminescence spectra of CDs at the different excitation wavelength, (c) bar diagram of different metal ions, and (d) bar diagram of interfering metal ions.

(a) Quantum yield calculation graph of CDs in the absence and presence of Cd2+ ions, (b) the effect of synthesis temperature, (c) effect of reaction time, and (d) fluorescence stability of the synthesized CDs.
(a) XRD, (b) FTIR spectrum, (c) HRTEM image at low resolution (50 nm) [inset particles size distribution diagram], and (d) HRTEM image at high resolution (20 nm) [Inset DLS analysis] of CDs.
(a) XPS spectrum, and (b) O 1s, (c) C 1s, (d) N 1s high resolution XPS spectra of the CDs.
(a) Fluorescence quenching spectra of CDs [after addition of different concentration (0.01-10 μM) of Cd2+ ions] and (b) Stern-Volmer equation.
(a) Influencing impact of pH on the CDs and (b) fluorescence spectra of CDs in different salt solution [0.5-2.5 M].
(a) lifetime decays, and (b) The Zeta potential of CDs after each addition of Cd2+ ions.
Fluorometric detection of Cd2+ ions in two different water samples (a) Tap water, and (b) pond water.
Graphical representation for the preparation of CDs from curry leaves and used for the selective detection of Cd2+ ions.
The comparison among the proposed method and other reported methods of Cd2+ ions detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: (Me4BOPHY)@N, N-bis (pyridine-2-ylmethyl) benzeneamine: Tetramethyl substitutedbis (difluoro boron)-1,2-bis[(1H-Pyrrol-2-yl)methylene] hydrazine modified with N,N-bis (pyridine-2-yl methyl) benzenzmine.
ZnS:Mn@PAMAM-OHG=3:Hydroxy-terminated poly (amidoamine) dendrimer in methanol of third generation.
Cd-IIP: Nanostructured Cadmium (ii) ion imprinted polymer.
N-G composite film-Bi electrode: Graphene nanosheet dispersed in Nafion-Gsolution used in situ plated Bismuth film electrode
NH2/SnO2-RTILL: Amine functionalized porous SnO2 nanowire bundles-room temperature ionic liquid nanocomposite.
SWCNTs@cystein: Single-walled carbon nanotubes covalently functionalized with cysteine.
CDTE QDs: Thioglycollic acid capped- CdTe quantum dots.

head.ce:title: Single Step Green Synthesis of Carbon Dots from Murraya koenigii leaves; A Unique Turn-off Fluorescent contrivance for Selective Sensing of Cd (II) ion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology (Indian School of Mines), Dhanbad, 826004, Jharkhand, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Indian Institute of Technology (Indian School of Mines)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: a Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology (Indian School of Mines), Dhanbad 826004, Jharkhand, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cadmium ion is the most toxic metal ion among all the hazardous metals; so, its detection is a crucial area for research and diagnosis. In this article, we have synthesized carbon dots (CDs) from natural source [ Murraya koenigii (Curry leaves) leaves] by the hydrothermal method for selective and sensitive detection of Cd2+ ions. The fluorescence quenching of CDs was due to ligand-metal charge transfer (LMCT). The synthesized CDs can donate electrons pair to the excited state vacant d-orbitals of the Cd2+ ions and form a chelate and diminishes the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. The synthesized CDs were characterized by Photoluminescence, UV-visible spectra, zeta potential, XRD, FTIR, HR-TEM, XPS, DLS, and life-time decay analysis. The CDs was highly selective towards Cd2+ ions sensing with broad linear range (0.01-8 μM) and limit of detection 0.29 nM. More importantly, this synthesized CDs successfully detect the Cd2+ ions in real water samples, and also shows high selectivity to Cd2+ ions even in the occurrence of other interfering metal ions.

ce:section.ce:para: The environmental pollution by heavy toxic metal ions raises serious issues for human health [1,2]. The cadmium ion (Cd2+) is considered the most poisonous metal ion among the heavy metal ions [3]. It is commonly used in dying [4], electroplating, agriculture [1], and semiconductor industries [5]. The limit of Cd2+ ion for drinking water is 3 ppb, according to the world health organization (WHO) [6]. But, uptake of cadmiferous species over tolerance limits from the environment would cause several diseases such as prostate, lungs, bones, and abdominal cancer [7]. Meanwhile, Cd2+ ion can accumulate in the food chain so, it is desirable to develop some analytical methods for selective and sensitive detection of Cd2+ ion in the environment and living cells. Recently, various analytical methods have been developed, including atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [8], electrochemical [9], optical [10], and mass spectroscopy [11] for the analysis of Cd2+ ion. Nowadays, a large number of fluorescence sensors have been developed to detect different types of heavy metal ions due to their high sensitivity, cost-effective, and real-time detection [12–18]. Here, few fluorescence sensors have been reported for the detection of the Cd2+ ions such as Campos et al. developed Zn nanoparticles functionalized by PAMAM-OH dendrimer fluorescent sensor for the cadmium detection [19]. Peng et al. synthesized a methionine-capped gold nanocluster fluorescent sensor for Cd2+ ions sensing [20]. Zhou et al. prepared a zinc ions-based fluorescent sensor for the detection of cadmium ion [12]. Zhao et al. developed an On/off fluorescence sensor for Cd2+ ions sensing [13]. Wong et al. synthesized a carbon dots from papaya for fluorometric detection of Iron (III) and Escherichia coli [18]. More importantly, Cd2+ ions sensing was the biggest challenge in the presence of other interfering transition metal ions, particularly Zn2+ ion. They have similar properties and in the same group of the periodic table. So, usually observed that Cd2+ and Zn2+ ion has identical fluorescence changes with changes fluorescence intensity of fluorophore [13]. Hence, there is a great need for synthesized a fluorophore can differentiate Cd2+ion from Zn2+ ion with high selectivity and sensitivity.

Nowadays, green chemistry has become more and more attractive because of its clean and sustainable features [21–23]. The preparation of carbon dots using economical and green materials is a critical issue that should be taken into consideration in a green synthetic strategy [21–23]. Herein, we synthesize carbon dots a new fluorescence sensor from the Curry leavesby the hydrothermal method [24–27]for Cd2+ ion sensing without any modification or functionalization. These synthesized carbon dots have low-cytotoxicity, easily available, low-cost, environmentally friendly, and facile synthesis processes compared to the carbon dots synthesized from the chemical sources [21–27]. The selective detection of Cd2+ ion was due to the ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) process [28,29]. The fluorescence quenching via LMCT was due to the amine group of carbon dots that can donate an electron pair to excited state vacant orbital of the Cd2+ ions [30]. The Curry leaves possess exciting compositions such as Carbazole, Girinimbine, Mahanimbine, Murrayanine, Isomahanine, Koenoline, Ethyl 2-phenyl, and Styrene [31]. The prepared CDs are extremely selective and sensitive towards Cd2+ ions in aqueous solution with a wide concentration range of 0.01-8 μM, with the limit of detection 0.29 nM. These synthesized carbon dots are also used in the sensing of Cd2+ ions in real water samples as well as used in the selective detection of Cd2+ ions even in the occurrence of interfering metal ions.

The naturally occurring curry leaves were used as a carbon source to synthesize highly fluorescent carbon dots by using a hydrothermal method [24–26], as shown in Scheme 1 . In summary, the fresh 10 g Curry leaves were mixed-up with 30 mL of distilled water, then transferred it into Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (50 mL). Then, the mixture solution was heated at a constant temperature at 180 °C for 4 h. After that, the autoclave was left for cooled-down at room temperature, and then the dark-brownish color solution was obtained. Then, the prepared solution (dark-brownish) was collected and filtered by filter paper to remove the unreacted particles then the filtrate was dialyzed against distilled water in a dialysis bag (3500 Da) for 24 h. Then the dialyzed solution was collected and stored at below 4 °C for further applications.

The absorbance of UV-visible absorption spectra was captured on a spectrophotometer (model–Perkin Elmer 950). The Fluorescence spectrum was performed on the fluorescence spectrometer (model - Perkin Elmer LS 55). The XRD (X-ray diffraction) pattern of CDs was obtained on an X-ray diffractometer (model - Phillips PW 1710). The FTIR (Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy) spectrum was captured on Thermo - Nicolet Nexus FTIR model 870 FTIR spectrophotometer. The High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) data were carried out using (Hitachi, H-600A). The measurement of XPS (X–Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy) was captured on PHI 5000 Versa. The lifetime decay of fluorescence of the CDs was measured on Fluorometer (model - Fluoro Max-4, France). The DLS (dynamic light scattering) and Zeta potential of CDs were measured and recorded by Nano ZS (Malvern) instrument.

The reference solution (0.1 molar H2SO4, with QY = 0.54 of quinine sulphate solution) was taken for calculation of quantum yield of synthesized CDs by using the formulae as mentioned below [24–29]. The quantum yield of the synthesized CDs was 5.4 % at an excited wavelength of 390 nm, as shown in Fig. 2(a).
QCDs = QR. ICDs/IR. AR/ACDs. η2 CDs/η2 R
Here, Q indicates the quantum yield of fluorescence, I indicate the intensity of fluorescence, η indicates the solvent’s refractive index used in this experiment, A represents the absorbance, R is the reference (of sample) and CDs indicates the carbon dots.

The fluorometric sensing of Cd2+ ions was performed in distilled water at room temperature. In this investigation, 10 μM (1 mL) of Cd2+ ions were mixed with CDs (1 μg/mL) solution and stirring for 15 min at room temperature. Then, the fluorescence emission spectrum (450 nm) of the mixture solutions was observed at excitation wavelength 390 nm. The Cd2+ ion’s selectivity was confirmed by addition of same amount of dissimilar metal salts (Ni2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Al3+, K+, Na+, Sn2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Cd2+,Zn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+) solutions into the CDs solution. The fluorescence spectra of all these solutions were recorded at excitation wavelength 390 nm, and fluorescence intensity of CDs was recorded lowest in Cd2+ ions, which confirmed the selectivity of Cd2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and analysis of water sample
ce:section.ce:para: The water samples were collected from two different sources (pond and tap water). The pond water was collected from the local lake (Rani Bandh, Dhanbad), and tap water was obtained from our laboratory IIT (ISM) Dhanbad. First of all, the solid suspension and other impurities of pond water were removed by filter paper (Whatman 42 filter paper). The tap water was used without any filtration. The different concentrations (10, 20, & 30 nM) of Cd2+ ions were added into pond water and tap water, and their fluorescence spectra were measured at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm.

The UV-visible spectrum indicates a single absorption band at 345 nm due to n-π* electronic transitions of carboxylic groups present on the surface of CDs. After that, the addition of Cd2+ ions, the absorption band was shifted to lower wavelength (hypsochromic shift) 335 nm, as shown in Fig. 1 (a). This hypsochromic shift was due to complexion formation between the Cd 2+ ions and carboxylic & amine groups present on the surface of the CDs [36]. Another likely to be the reason was the energy gap between the ground state and excited state of the metal bounded CDs get increased. So, the possible ligand-to-metal charges transfer (LMCT), and chelation between CDs and Cd2+ ions drive the equilibrium towards the blue-shift [36]. The emission spectra (excitation-dependent) of obtained CDs are depicted in Fig. 1(b). The excitation wavelengths of prepared CDs are varied from 330 nm to 410 nm; the emission intensities were shifted to higher wavelength, and maximum emission intensity is found at 450 nm at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm. The maximum fluorescence intensity at 450 nm was observed; because of at this particular wavelength, a large number of particles will be excited. Simultaneously, the selectivity of Cd2+ ions was confirmed by the addition of different metal ions (Ni2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Al3+, K+, Na+, Sn2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+) solution into the CDs solution at excitation wavelength 390 nm, as shown in Fig. 1(c). The plotted histogram indicates that the fluorescence intensity of CDs was lowest in the presence of Cd2+ ions in comparison to other metal ions. This indicates that the synthesized CDs are highly selective towards Cd2+ ions in comparison to the other metal ions. This selectivity was attributable to the vacant d-orbitals of Cd2+ ions have a high binding affinity towards the carboxyl and amino groups of CDs as compare to the other metal ions. This selectivity towards Cd2+ ions was due to CDs that can donate the electron pair to the excited state vacant d-orbitals of Cd2+ ions via non-radiative electrons transfer process and form a surface complexion [29–30].

The aqueous solution of CDs shows the brownish color under daylight, blue fluorescent intensity under UV light (at 365 nm). Then the addition of Cd2+ ions diminished the fluorescent color of CDs, as shown in Scheme 1. We have also prepared the CDs at different ranges of temperature (160 °C-200 °C); the maximum emission peak was observed at 180 °C because, at this temperature large number of particles will be carbonised, as shown in Fig. 2 (b). Simultaneously, we have also studied the different preparation time (2, 4, and 6 h); the maximal fluorescence intensity of the CDs was located at 4 h, as shown in Fig. 2(c). Similarly, we have checked the fluorescence stability property of CDs with respect to time, there is no noticeable change in the emission spectra of CDs after synthesis of one month, as shown in Fig. 2(d).

The broad diffraction peak of CDs was displayed on the XRD pattern, as shown in Fig. 3 (a) A broad amorphous peak was obtained at 2ɵ = 23° because of the small size of the CDs, and corresponding spacing of lattice (002). Thus, the results confirmed that the nature of CDs was amorphous [27]. The amorphous property is due to CDs possessing closely packed C-N, C-C, C = C and C = O bonds of CDs.

The combined absorption band arises in the FTIR spectrum at 3425.3 cm-1 due to phenolic and amine groups present on the surface of the CDs. The presence of an aliphatic carbon compound and their stretching frequencies of -CH give two peaks 2903.4 cm-1 and 2341.7 cm-1. The aromatic rings are present in CDs having C-O and C-C stretching displays FTIR peaks at 1638.4 cm-1, and 1489.7 cm-1, respectively. Furthermore, the absorption peak of constituent ether linkage and -C-N stretching in CDs was obtained at 1119.5 cm-1 and 1379.0 cm-1, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3(b).

The particle size and shape of CDs were investigated by TEM analysis, depicted in Fig. 3(c). It manifestly shows that the carbon dots are spherical in shape and particle size ranging from 2-8 nm, as shown in Fig. 3(c), and it correlates with the narrow size distribution [Inset of Figure-3(c)]. At the same time, HRTEM also confirms that CDs are spherical, as given in Fig. 3(d). Simultaneously, DLS analysis revealed that the hydrodynamic size of the CDs and result shows that the particle size of the CDs is about 110 nm is due to the accumulation of the particles in the aqueous medium, as expressed in inset Fig. 3(d).

XPS spectra analysed the electronic states and chemical composition of the synthesized CDs. The XPS peaks were obtained at 283.28 eV for element C1 s, 399.25 eV for N1 s, and 530.39 eV for O1 s [32], as shown in Fig. 4 (a). The above results revealed that the synthesized CDs contain carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen with an atomic percentage of 62.6 %. 30.4 % and 7.0 %, respectively [32]. The scanning report of high resolutions spectra of two different chemical environments for C-OH/C-O-C and C = O obtained at 530.14 eV and 531.49 eV respectively of O1 s region, as shown in Fig. 4(b). The four different chemical environments for the high-resolution scan of C1S are shown in Fig. 4(c). The different XPS peaks in C1 s spectra corresponds to the sp2 of C = C obtained at 283.12 eV, sp3 of C-N at 282.70 eV, C-O at 286.32 eV and O-C = O at 284.34 eV [32]. The N1S high-resolution spectra correspond to different chemicals environment for C-N-C and N-H were obtained at 399.84 eV and 398.08 eV, respectively [32,33], as shown in Fig. 4(d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cd2+ ions detection by fluorometric method
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorometric detection of Cd2+ ions by the CDs is shown in Fig. 5 (a). The fluorescence intensity of CDs was gradually decreasing with increased in the concentration of Cd2+ ions (0.01–10 μM) at excitation wavelength 390 nm. The concentration of Cd2+ ions was reached at 10μM; the maximum intensity of fluorescence of CDs was quenched. The concentration of Cd2+ ions increases, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was progressively decreased and shifted towards a lower wavelength of 460 nm to 450 nm. This shifting may be due to the gap of energy between the ground and excited state of the bounded metal CDs get increased [34,35]. So that, the possible ligand–metal charge transfer (LMCT), and chelation between CDs and Cd2+ ions drives the equilibrium towards the blue-shift [34,35]. The calibration plot was plotted between the fluorescence intensity of CDs against the concentration of Cd2+ ions, as depicted in Fig. 5(b). The calibration curve exhibited as F/F0 = 28 [Cd2+ ions] + 154 was raised up to the range of concentration (1–8 μM) with correlation coefficient (R2) = 0.99, as depicted in [Fig. 5(b)]. The LOD (limit of detection) value was calculated to be 0.29 nM, based on 3SD/k (SD stands for the standard deviation of a blank signal of CDs and k stands for a calibration curve slope). This is comparable to the formerly reported values shown in Table 1 .

Finally, we have studied the particular assay by using possible interference of representative metal ions (Ni2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Al3+, K+, Na+, Sn2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+) under same condition. The fluorescence intensity of CDs was not affected after the addition of representative metal ions. Then after the addition of Cd2+ ions are added into the mixture solution drastic changed in the fluorescent intensity of mixture solution, as shown in Fig. 1(d). The CDs selectivity was confirmed in the presence of representative different metal ions, which caused no interference, therefore confirmed that this method could be applied for the selective detection of Cd2+ ions in the presence of representative metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The pH and ionic strengths stability of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Effect on the fluorescence intensity of the synthesized CDs after the addition of different buffer solutions (pH = 2-14), as depicted in Fig. 6 (a). The fluorescent intensity of synthesized CDs first increases at (pH 2-9) and then decreased at higher pH (10-14), which shows that the fluorescence property of CDs is highly depended on pH value. These behaviors are due to phenolic (-OH) and carbonyl groups (C = O) that exist on the surface of the CDs. As the C = O groups behave like a positive charges carrier
C =
⊕
O
-H
in the pH range (pH 2-9), therefore, fluorescence intensity increases with increasing of the pH values. Large numbers of positive charge carriers are exist on the surface of CDs, which causes the electrostatic repulsion, and hence, it increased the fluorescence intensity. Then, the different concentrations of NaCl salt solution (0.5-2.5 mol/L) was added to the synthesized CDs and it happened that there was a slight change in the intensity of fluorescence after NaCl concentration was increased, as shown in Fig. 6(b). These results indicate that the CDs are stable under the condition of high ionic strength.

We speculate that the vacant d-orbital of Cd2+ ions might link with a carboxyl group or an amino group of CDs via ligand-to-metal charge transfer was confirmed by Zeta potential and fluorescent life-time decay analysis. The experiments of time corresponding single-photon (hʋ) counting of CDs were determined by fluorescence life-time decay analysis, as shown in Fig. 7 (a). This sensing mechanism followed the dynamic quenching because the concentration of Cd2+ ions changed the τ0/τ value [43,44]. The average life-time value of the CDs was obtained as 5.39 ns, and after the addition of Cd2+ ions, it decreased up to 2.31 ns at the wavelength of excitation 390 nm. The decrease in the average life-time value of CDs indicates that the dynamic quenching has occurred [43,44]. Because of this quenching process, all the fluorophores may collide with the quencher during their excited-state life-time, so both fluorescence intensity and average life-time are decreased. The decreased in absorption intensity and shifting in absorption wavelength of CDs in the presence of Cd2+ ions also confirms that the dynamic quenching has been occurred [45], as shown in Fig. 1(a). This dynamic mechanism is attributed to the ligand-to-metal charge transfer or be the formation of chelation between CDs and Cd2+ ions.

The Zeta potential analysis was used to characterize the surface charge of CDs, and their results were confirmed that the binding capacity of CDs and Cd2+ ions. These results were also revealed that CDs showed negative Zeta potential (l0.1 mV) because of the carboxylic groups present on the surface of CDs. Then, the addition of Cd2+ ions shows the value of Zeta potential was increased up to 5.2 mV, as shown in Fig. 7(b). This notable change in the Zeta potential value (10.1-5.2 mV) in the presence of 10 μM of Cd2+ ions at pH7, which confirms that the CDs was combined with the Cd2+ ions by the ligand-to-metal charge transfer [43,44].

The synthesized CDs were applied in the different water samples to detect the Cd2+ ions present in the water sample. Therefore the standard addition technique was used to find out the average value of fluorescence intensity with standard deviation. In this experiment, the Cd2+ ions were measured and prepared as samples in three levels, such as 5, 10, and 15 nM, which on further added to the threefold diluted water samples (collected from different sources). Then each sample was repeatedly analyzed three times and obtained the average value of fluorescence intensity with standard deviation. The comparative study of the efficiency of fluorescence spectra by different water samples containing the different concentrations of Cd2+ ions is shown in Fig. 8 (a) & (b). Each addition of Cd2+ ions in various water samples decreases the ratio of fluorescence intensity.

In brief, the fluorescent CDs of Murraya koinigii leaves were prepared successfully by the single-step hydrothermal process without using toxic chemicals. The preparation process for CDs is simple, cost-effective, green, and environment-friendly. The synthesized CDs have been used for sensitive and selective detection of Cd2+ ions with a limit of detection 0.29 nM. The aqueous solution of CDs emits intense blue color under UV-light with a quantum yield of 5.4 %. The probable sensing mechanism may be due ligand-to-metal charge transfer process. This sensing mechanism was supported by the UV-visible spectroscopy, life-time decay, and zeta potential analysis. The synthesized CDs possess outstanding water solubility, good biocompatibility, excellent chemical and photochemical properties, as well as inertness to the interference of different metal ions. To the best of our knowledge, this is for the first time that CDs have been synthesized from the Curry leaves and directly used for the detection of Cd2+ ions in real samples. This report will open a new avenue for the fabrication of high-performance sensing applications.

Subhash Chandra Pandey: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Visualization, Funding acquisition. Amit Kumar: Writing - review & editing. Sumanta Kumar Sahu: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research work was supported by Indian Institute of Technology (Indian School of Mines), Dhanbad (Jharkhand), India.
